The cities of the priests and Levites were intended not only to accommodate them but also to place them, as religious teachers, in various parts of the land. Although the typical service of the tabernacle or temple was confined to one location, the preaching of the word of God, prayer, and praise were not so limited. These cities were to be given from each tribe, with each tribe making a grateful acknowledgement to God. Each tribe benefited from having Levites dwelling among them to teach them about the Lord; thus, no areas of the country remained in darkness. The gospel teaches that whoever is taught in the word should share good things with those who teach, and we are to free God’s ministers from distracting cares so they can have leisure for their duties. This allows them to be fully employed in their work and use acts of kindness to gain people's goodwill and draw their attention.

To clearly show abhorrence of murder and ensure the punishment of murderers, the nearest relative of the deceased, known as the avenger of blood or redeemer of blood, could pursue and execute vengeance in obvious cases. A distinction is made not between sudden anger and malice aforethought—both are crimes of murder—but between intentionally striking a man with a weapon likely to cause death and an unintentional blow. Only in the latter case did the city of refuge offer protection. Murder in all forms pollutes a land. Sadly, many murders occur under the guise of duels or prize fights and go unpunished. There were six cities of refuge; one could be reached within less than a day’s journey from any part of the land. Man-slayers could flee to these cities for refuge and safety until they had a fair trial. If acquitted, they were protected from the avenger of blood but remained within the city limits until the high priest's death. This reminds us that the death of the great High Priest is the only means by which sins are pardoned and sinners set free. These cities are plainly alluded to in both the Old and New Testaments; we cannot doubt their typical character. 'Turn you to the strong hold, you prisoners of hope,' says the voice of mercy, referring to the city of refuge. St. Paul describes the great comfort of fleeing for refuge to the hope set before us, a passage often applied to the cities of refuge. The rich mercies of salvation through Christ, prefigured by these cities, demand our attention. 1. Did the ancient city raise its towers of safety? See Christ raised up on the cross; and is he not exalted at the right hand of his Father, to be a Prince and a Saviour, to give repentance and remission of sins? 2. Does not the highway of salvation resemble the smooth and plain path to the city of refuge? Survey the path that leads to the Redeemer. Is there any stumbling-block to be found therein, except that which an evil heart of unbelief supplies for its own fall? 3. Waymarks were set up pointing to the city. And is it not the office of the ministers of the gospel to direct sinners to Him? 4. The gate of the city stood open night and day. Has not Christ declared, 'He who comes unto me I will in no wise cast out'? 5. The city of refuge afforded support to everyone who entered its walls. Those who have reached the refuge may live by faith on Him whose flesh is meat indeed, and whose blood is drink indeed. 6. The city was a refuge for all. In the gospel there is no respect of persons. That soul does not live which deserves not Divine wrath; that soul does not live which may not in simple faith hope for salvation and life eternal through the Son of God.
